summary:
The Younger Dryas was a geologically brief but climatically severe cold interval that interrupted the warming trend at the end of the last glacial period, lasting from approximately 12,900 to 11,700 BCE. Named after Dryas octopetala, a cold-tolerant alpine flower whose pollen appears in abundance in sediment cores from this period, the Younger Dryas represents one of the most abrupt and dramatic climate shifts of the past 20,000 years, with temperatures in Greenland dropping by as much as 10–15 degrees Celsius within decades.

The mechanism behind the Younger Dryas is widely attributed to a disruption of the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) — the system of ocean currents that transports warm water northward and distributes heat across the North Atlantic. The trigger was likely the catastrophic drainage of glacial Lake Agassiz into the North Atlantic, flooding the ocean with vast quantities of cold fresh water that disrupted the salinity-driven circulation system. This effectively shut down or greatly weakened the "ocean conveyor belt," dramatically reducing the delivery of heat to northwestern Europe and the North Atlantic region.

The ecological and human consequences of the Younger Dryas were far-reaching. In the Levant and neighboring regions, the cold and dry conditions that accompanied the event stressed the ecosystems that the [[event:4|Natufian culture]] depended upon. At [[event:1252|Abu Hureyra]] in Syria, the Younger Dryas is associated with a brief abandonment of the site, followed by the adoption of cultivation when communities resettled — suggesting that climatic stress may have been a proximate driver of the agricultural transition. Across North America, Europe, and Asia, human populations adapted to sharply reduced resource availability through increased mobility, dietary diversification, and — in some regions — the early intensification of plant food use.

The end of the Younger Dryas around 11,700 BCE was as abrupt as its onset. Temperatures in the North Atlantic region rose by roughly 10 degrees Celsius in as little as a decade or two, triggering rapid ecosystem change and opening the way for the warming of the early Holocene — the climatic backdrop against which the [[event:1|Neolithic Revolution]] would unfold. The Younger Dryas thus stands as a pivotal geological event that helped set the conditions for the emergence of agriculture and, ultimately, civilization.

The event is also linked to ongoing debate about the extinction of Pleistocene megafauna. Some researchers associate the [[event:1289|megafauna extinction in the Americas]] and elsewhere with the combination of human hunting pressure and the ecological disruptions of the Younger Dryas, though the precise contributions of each factor remain debated.

The Younger Dryas has become a key reference point in paleoclimatology and a cautionary example of the speed and scale at which Earth's climate system can shift, with implications for understanding modern climate change.
